codeReview.summary: The implementation correctly replaces diff_scenes with change-list-driven damage tracking as specified. Build succeeds cleanly, 1490 tests pass, QEMU visual verification confirms typing and clock updates work. However, there is one correctness regression: when a node moves between frames (e.g., cursor repositioned via arrow key), only the NEW position is marked dirty. The OLD position retains stale pixels (cursor ghost). The previous diff_scenes approach correctly damaged both old and new positions by maintaining prev_nodes. The change-list approach has no access to previous positions, so moved-node old positions are never cleaned up until the next full repaint. This manifests as ghost cursors during arrow key navigation, cleared only on next clock tick coinciding with input (full rebuild), or next text edit (doc text damage covers old area).

issues.description: Missing old-position damage for moved nodes. The change list provides only current-frame node IDs. For each changed node, abs_bounds computes the NEW absolute position and adds it as a dirty rect. But when a node moves (e.g., cursor from line 1 to line 2 via arrow key), the OLD position needs to be repainted to erase stale pixels. The previous diff_scenes code handled this: `rects.push(old_rect); if old_rect != new_rect { rects.push(new_rect); }`. The new code only damages the new position. Fix options: (a) store previous bounds per-node in compositor (~8 KiB for [i32;4] × MAX_NODES), damage both old and new bounds; (b) have core include 'invalidated rects' in the change list; (c) for cursor-only moves, also mark the parent N_DOC_TEXT as changed (large damage rect but correct).

The `None` arm in `dr.change_list()` match (line 454) should be unreachable because `dr.is_full_repaint()` is checked on line 431, and both read the same FULL_REPAINT sentinel. If is_full_repaint() returns false, change_list() will return Some(...). The None arm is good defensive code but could benefit from a comment noting it's a defensive fallback for potential TOCTOU between the two reads (each independently selects the front buffer via front_of()).

sharedStateObservations.observation: The abs_bounds function does not account for Node::scroll_y when computing absolute positions. This is currently correct because core pre-applies scroll to node positions for incremental updates (scroll_y=0 in scene graph, confirmed at scene_state.rs:267-269). However, this implicit dependency is not documented — if a future change sets scroll_y != 0 for incremental updates, abs_bounds would produce incorrect damage rects. The AGENTS.md 'Data buffer strategy' section should note: 'abs_bounds assumes scroll_y=0 for all incremental update nodes. Core must pre-apply scroll to viewport-relative coordinates.'
sharedStateObservations.evidence: scene/lib.rs abs_bounds() walks parent chain summing x,y but ignores scroll_y. core/scene_state.rs:267-269 sets scroll_y=0 with comment 'core pre-applies scroll to run positions'.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The feature's expectedBehavior states 'Visual output identical to pre-change' but visual verification only tested typing and clock updates, not cursor-only movement (arrow keys). The skill procedure says 'Send keystrokes to exercise the feature' but doesn't specify testing cursor movement separately from text insertion. AGENTS.md or the skill could add a verification checklist item: 'For damage-tracking changes, verify cursor-only moves (arrow keys) do not leave ghost artifacts.'
sharedStateObservations.evidence: Handoff interactiveChecks show only typing ('helloworld') and clock update tests. Arrow key navigation was not tested. The cursor ghost regression was not caught.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The diff_scenes function in scene/lib.rs is no longer called by the compositor but is still used by 6 test cases in test/tests/scene.rs. It's not dead code. If the change-list approach becomes the sole damage mechanism, diff_scenes may be useful as a reference/oracle for correctness testing (e.g., comparing change-list damage rects against diff_scenes output for the same frame pair).
sharedStateObservations.evidence: grep shows diff_scenes used at scene.rs:1651,1669,1697,1724,1751,1791 — all test assertions.

summary: The implementation correctly replaces diff_scenes with change-list-driven damage tracking, removing the ~40 KiB prev_nodes heap allocation and O(N) byte comparison. All code paths (full repaint, partial change list, empty change list, overflow sentinel, node count change) are handled. However, there is a blocking correctness regression: node movements (cursor repositioning via arrow keys) only damage the new position, leaving stale pixels at the old position. The previous diff_scenes approach damaged both old and new positions. The fix requires the compositor to track previous node bounds (lightweight, ~8 KiB) and damage both old and new positions for each changed node.
